title: Google I/O 2025 – NYtQuneZMXc
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] JAMES MANYIKA: Welcome back,
everybody, and thank you for coming to this session. I hope you're all having
a wonderful time here at I/O. One of the things we
think a lot about at Google when we think about
the impact of AI, we think about how it's going
to advance the possibilities for people, individuals. We also think about how it can
enable and power the economy. We also think a
lot about how much it can advance science and help
us address pressing challenges for society. Now, you heard a little
bit of this on the stage yesterday from Demis. But this session, we
wanted to spend more time focusing on that
last part, which is how we see AI advancing
and driving major landmark advances in science and
helping us tackle some pressing societal challenges. We have extraordinary
teams across Google, in Google Research,
Google DeepMind, work of research
scientists and others working on these hard
scientific problems that are important for
society and for humanity. And I'm thrilled that I have
several colleagues here. Pushmeet, I'll
introduce each of them properly when we start
the conversation. But Pushmeet, Joelle,
and our friend and collaborator Anima
Anandkumar from Caltech. So why don't we jump into it? By the way, if you
want to find out more about our AI
and science work, I think there's a QR
code that's available. It'll give you a
lot more details about some of the
extraordinary work that my colleagues and all
of us are doing at Google. Let me start with you, Pushmeet. So let me actually
introduce Pushmeet. So Pushmeet Kohli is
the Vice President for Science and Strategic
Initiatives at Google DeepMind. He works on a lot of the
foundational research advancing science across multiple areas--
biology, mathematics, physics, and so forth. But let's get into it, Pushmeet. A lot has happened
in the last year. What are some of the
things you're most excited about that you and your
colleagues have worked on? PUSHMEET KOHLI: So I think,
James, one of the first things
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – NYtQuneZMXc
text:
But let's get into it, Pushmeet. A lot has happened
in the last year. What are some of the
things you're most excited about that you and your
colleagues have worked on? PUSHMEET KOHLI: So I think,
James, one of the first things that we have to talk about
when we are talking about AI for science is AlphaFold. Because it's, in
some sense, being a key milestone and
a key example of what is the potential
of AI for science. What AlphaFold has achieved
is solve this amazing problem in biology that has implications
across a whole spectrum of different application
areas, from drug discovery to developing enzymes
for decomposing plastics, to really uncovering a lot of
mysteries of synthetic biology. Now, what AlphaFold 2 did was
find the structures of proteins. Last year, we expanded
that with AlphaFold 3, which goes beyond the
structures of proteins and sheds light on the structures of
all the other biomolecules in the body like how do proteins
interact with small ligands, which are very relevant
for drug discovery. How do they form complexes? What is the structure
of those complexes? How do proteins interact with
nucleic acids, RNA and DNA? So it really expands
our ability to think about structural
characterization of biology. Biology, in itself, is one of
the most amazing application areas for AI, given the
complexity that it has. And what we have been able to
do is build on top of AlphaFold to not only make progress
on structural biology, but take on many
other different tasks. So two good examples in
biology have been our work on understanding the genome. What is the effect of
missense variants or mutations in the genome? So we had a model
called AlphaMissense, which was built on top
of AlphaFold, which is able to predict
whether variants or mutations in the genome
would be pathogenic or benign. And this has already
seeing impact on diagnosis of rare diseases
in the medical and clinical context. Another example is
AlphaProteo, which again leverages
AlphaFold 3 and is now able to think about,
how do you synthesize
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – NYtQuneZMXc
text:
And this has already
seeing impact on diagnosis of rare diseases
in the medical and clinical context. Another example is
AlphaProteo, which again leverages
AlphaFold 3 and is now able to think about,
how do you synthesize binders for new proteins. So this is coming up with
completely novel proteins that do not exist in nature
that we have purposefully designed to solve certain
tasks, like binding to specific targets. And that opens up a variety
of different applications, both for drug discovery as well
as for many other application areas as well. JAMES MANYIKA: What
I find extraordinary, and I think you all
know this, so AlphaFold is our system that won the
Nobel Prize that Demis and John Jumper got a Nobel Prize for. But what I find
extraordinary is the fact that now more than 2.5 million
biologists around the world are using it. And these are people
in over 190 countries. And I think that's
extraordinary how it opens up the possibilities for science. But, Joelle, let me come to you. So I should introduce Joelle. So Joelle is a Senior
Director at Google DeepMind. She works on a lot of
the foundational research in healthcare and the
medical space in particular. She also leads a broad range
of teams across Google, building extraordinary
applications and solutions. What have you been most excited
about in the last year, Joelle? JOELLE BARRAL: Well, thanks,
James, and thanks for having me. Hi, everyone. It's really great to
be here this morning. It's a tough question. Because we do so much across
the spectrum in healthcare. I think we've
published more than 50, 5-0 papers last year alone. But if you're asking
me to pick one, I think I'll cheat a
little bit because I'll talk about a family of papers
around AMIE, our Articulate Medical Intelligence Explorer. So you can think of it
as a large language model that we've specifically designed
to be able to do diagnosis. So in some ways, augmenting or
replacing what a PCP would do, what a physician would do. And this is obviously
a research project.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – NYtQuneZMXc
text:
So you can think of it
as a large language model that we've specifically designed
to be able to do diagnosis. So in some ways, augmenting or
replacing what a PCP would do, what a physician would do. And this is obviously
a research project. We're really
exploring the art of the possible with
that research project. A year ago, we had done
maybe the initial paper. That was a very simple
dialogue system, text only. And over the last
year we, for example, looked at what it could
do in specialty care, both in cardiology
and in oncology, with our colleagues at Stanford
and at Houston Methodist. So we're working with
physicians and clinical teams to see how, for
specialty, when you really need a lot more knowledge,
our language models can also do diagnostic reasoning
in a way that is on par or better than physicians. We've also continued to
push that series of paper to give eyes and ears to AMIE. So not just have things
that are text only, but also have the ability
to upload various documents and reason on those documents. And then last but
not least, we've started a study with Beth
Israel Medical Center to be able to see what happens
when you start deploying this in clinical settings, again,
under IRB as a research project. But to see under
physician supervision when this is able
to either accelerate or augment the diagnosis a
physician would be doing. So a lot of exciting progress
with AMIE over the last year. JAMES MANYIKA: And
actually, I know exactly why you found it difficult
perhaps to pick one, because there's so much. One of the things, at
least, I was particularly thrilled about last year was
some of the work in tuberculosis diagnosis, for example, using
AI tools, just because, what? Something like 30% to 40% of the
world's population that has TB goes undiagnosed. And often that's because
they live in places where the resources
are not available. So that was extraordinary. So Anima, again,
I keep forgetting to introduce my panelists. So Anima is a professor of
computer science at Caltech.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – NYtQuneZMXc
text:
And often that's because
they live in places where the resources
are not available. So that was extraordinary. So Anima, again,
I keep forgetting to introduce my panelists. So Anima is a professor of
computer science at Caltech. And she's done some of the most
groundbreaking work around AI, advancing how we
understand physical systems through these incredible
techniques called neural operators. But you should talk
about what have you been most excited
about in the work you've done in the last year? ANIMA ANANDKUMAR:
Thank you, James. And thank you for
having me here. It's such an
exciting time for AI. And to me, the
passion has always been how we can advance
pretty much all of science and build broad
frameworks and foundations to be able to do that. And to me, now we are seeing the
entire scientific method itself getting upended with AI. Because the traditional
approach has been, scientists have ideas,
hypothesis, you go test it, you get feedback. You come back, and you
redo the whole process. And of course, now with
approaches like Co-Scientist, AI can come up with
all kinds of new ideas. But the bottleneck still
remains that if you had to go into the lab and
test, build, fabricate, do this for every single
hypothesis or idea, it would still be not feasible. Because that's the
critical bottleneck. And so what we've
been building is AI that understands the
physical world, that can really create very accurate,
high fidelity, digital twins of these
physical systems. To give you an
example, we are now able to design photonic
devices in the digital realm. And the question is, can we
do that with enough confidence that we can overcome the need
for lots of lab experiments so we can go ahead and come up
with what the best design is, given all the
different manufacturing and other constraints? And for the domain scientists
that are collaborating with us, this is just an
amazing new possibility that they never thought
was ever realistic. Because the
traditional approach is we already have
a current device.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – NYtQuneZMXc
text:
And for the domain scientists
that are collaborating with us, this is just an
amazing new possibility that they never thought
was ever realistic. Because the
traditional approach is we already have
a current device. Let's tweak a few parameters. And again, it's too risky to
change too much of the design, so we'll do small tweaks. And we hope it gets better. But the challenge is the lab
experiments also don't give you gradients or feedback, so you
don't know if it didn't work, why not. Whereas if you can create these
now surrogates or models that accurately capture that physical
phenomena and these AI models are differentiable, we can even
explore the entire design space and come up with what we think
are compelling and the most promising designs. And we were earlier
able to do this for designing the
medical catheter, where AI was able to
understand the fluid flow and come up with an
optimized design that reduced bacterial
contamination the maximum. And we only had to go to
the lab once and test it, and it led to
hundred-fold reduction in bacterial contamination. And so that to me,
is the promise. As much as we can design
in the digital realm, with all of its physical
complexities and details so that it just works
in the physical world. And this is where the techniques
of neural operators, in my view, are critical. Because the physical world
is not just the visible. So when we think
of the world model, we're thinking of
the world we see. But there is the world
of the invisible, which happens at so many scales. You can think of fluids
moving, matter deforming. If you go down even further to
the quantum effects at these scales that human
eyes cannot see. So there are different
levels of those complexity you need to capture for
different physical phenomena, for different scientific areas. And the approach
of neural operators can really help us learn
across these different scales. Because it's not limited to one
fixed resolution or one fixed grid, which standard
neural networks do.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – NYtQuneZMXc
text:
for different scientific areas. And the approach
of neural operators can really help us learn
across these different scales. Because it's not limited to one
fixed resolution or one fixed grid, which standard
neural networks do. And hence, they can
learn any phenomena of any complexity of any
different multiple scales. And that's what a lot of
physical phenomena are. JAMES MANYIKA: I think it's
quite extraordinary when you think about the generality
of these possibilities, but also the idea
that we go away from solving problems one at a
time and look at all of them. And I'm struck by
that in your work. But also, Pushmeet, what
you were describing earlier with things even
AlphaMissense, where I think before
AlphaMissense, scientists were going one at a time, in
terms of those genetic coding misspellings. They had only
understood like 1%. And then with AlphaMissense,
you got that up to 71% or 70% of all the missense spelling. So this idea of doing things
at scale is extraordinary. But, Pushmeet, one area you
didn't talk a lot about, and you've been doing
and your colleagues have been doing a lot of work, is
on weather and climate science, which is another
physical system. Say more about some
of the advances in the last year in weather. PUSHMEET KOHLI: So I think
weather and meteorology and climate, I
think these are some of the very important topics
that we are seeing a lot that is happening with AI. First of all, there is a lot
of conventional computing that has been
applied to weather. It's not as if computational
methods have not been applied to weather. There has been a
lot of investment. Weather prediction
has been the subject of supercomputer research. There's a lot of computation
that goes into it. What we have been able to show
in the last couple of years, in fact, have been that AI
models, neural network based models, have been able to
produce near-term forecasts that are not only more
accurate than these very expensive simulations,
but they can be done in a minute on a single chip.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – NYtQuneZMXc
text:
in fact, have been that AI
models, neural network based models, have been able to
produce near-term forecasts that are not only more
accurate than these very expensive simulations,
but they can be done in a minute on a single chip. That democratizes
weather prediction in a way that has
not been seen before. Now you don't need
a supercomputer to make weather prediction. You can do that on a single GPU
or a single TPU in a minute. So that opens up
the possibilities. And what we have done is
basically with our WeatherNext models, we are actually
making these predictions available across the whole
spectrum of applications. So people have now signed
up through Google Cloud to access these predictions. And our new model,
WeatherNext Gen, has been able to show that you
can track things like cyclones much, much more accurately
over longer time scales than conventional systems. And then finally, work happening
across Google, Google Research and DeepMind, on NeuralGCM,
these global models, which are similar to, in fact,
what Anima has been developing. They have been able to not
only produce good estimates about whether in the short term,
but also speed up computations about climate simulations. So also, what are
the implications of these systems over the
long-term horizons of climate prediction? So we are seeing developments
across the whole spectrum of the time domain, from
near-term forecasting, medium-term forecasting, as well
as long-term climate prediction. JAMES MANYIKA: I think
it's extraordinary. It also reminds me of
some of the work we've been doing recently,
as you know, related to climate around
wildfires, for example. So I don't know if
folks in the room know. So for example, we've been doing
wildfire boundary predictions now in about 27 countries, which
is extraordinarily helpful. In fact, some of
these techniques were used in the Palisades
fires to actually track the boundaries. And very recently, we actually
launched this new system, FireSat, where it's going
to be a constellation of 53 satellites.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – NYtQuneZMXc
text:
In fact, some of
these techniques were used in the Palisades
fires to actually track the boundaries. And very recently, we actually
launched this new system, FireSat, where it's going
to be a constellation of 53 satellites. The first one went up. And the idea with that
is that up until now, the granularity of what
you could actually look at was fairly coarse. But with these
satellites, we'll be able to take a look at areas
that are like 5 by 5 meters and be able to make
predictions anywhere on Earth. So that's another area
related to climate. But just to shift
gears a little bit. One of the things that's
been interesting is, in addition to these
kind of landmark advances that you've all
described, the methods and the ways in which
scientists do their work are also changing as a
result of all of this. And I was wondering,
maybe, Joelle, you could start this off. But can you talk a
little bit about how you see the actual
work of doing science changing as a result of AI? JOELLE BARRAL: Yeah, absolutely. I think at a meta
level, we are really seeing how AI can
completely change the way we're doing science. And if you think of what we've
published as the Co-Scientist, the idea is to have a
system, an AI system that has been specifically
designed to help scientists in their work. So think of it as
a powerful system that can first, for
example, help you understand the whole literature-- not just what has been
published recently here, but what has been
published globally, anywhere over the
last 100 years, and really put all of that
into perspective with respect to the question you're
currently asking yourself. It can then help you
formulate various hypotheses. And that's really leveraging
the creativity of large language models. But it doesn't stop there. Once it has all of
those hypotheses, it can also help you, as a
scientist, explore each of them and critique them,
again leveraging AI. So it will rank them. You can make them
compete with each other. And it improves
itself so that it
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – NYtQuneZMXc
text:
Once it has all of
those hypotheses, it can also help you, as a
scientist, explore each of them and critique them,
again leveraging AI. So it will rank them. You can make them
compete with each other. And it improves
itself so that it starts, as you interact
with the system, as again, as a scientist,
it's not doing it on its own. But you're really helping. I like to think of it as a new
chalkboard or a whiteboard. It's something
where you can really bounce ideas and experiment
with, but in a much, much more knowledgeable way
and really augment what you can do very quickly. So then, similar to
what you were saying, you're narrowing down
the number of things you really have to go to
the lab with to experiment. And so it's what Demis
likes to speak about as science at digital speed. Because you can do so much
based on just the information you have and those tools. JAMES MANYIKA: Actually,
say a little bit more about the experience
of Co-Scientist. Because one of the things
that was extraordinary to me, at least, was the fact
that in Co-Scientist, we were able to explore
new areas, whether it's in drug repurposing,
for example, or antimicrobial resistance,
and actually discover interesting new insights. Say more about Co-Scientist. JOELLE BARRAL:
Yeah, no, precisely. I think with this system,
it's an agentic platform. So you have a couple
of stages where it's looking at, for
example, for a given drug, how can it be repurposed. And you see that for
that particular problem, you have a combinatorial space
that you have to search through. And that's typically
a problem for which AI is very well suited. And so you take this question,
and you let the system explore. And what we've
been able to do is compare what the AI
comes with with what has happened in the
field and rediscover things that had taken labs
years sometimes to get to. And so it's still
in the early days. I think we still have to
do a lot of validation to make sure that
none of those things where, in the training
data, none of those things
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – NYtQuneZMXc
text:
things that had taken labs
years sometimes to get to. And so it's still
in the early days. I think we still have to
do a lot of validation to make sure that
none of those things where, in the training
data, none of those things could have been, contaminated
in any way, shape, or form. So we are really
continuing the work to really see moving
forward, if we have with the Co-Scientist
a particular hypothesis or if it's really, truly making
us do a discovery that we wouldn't have come
up before, that we do that in a way that
is also extremely rigorous in its validation. So I also want to
really emphasize that, again, everything
is moving so quickly. We also have to
continue to have a very rigorous scientific method
to make sure that we truly have something unique in the
way we can do new discoveries. JAMES MANYIKA: Anima, how do
you think this is changing how scientists do their work? ANIMA ANANDKUMAR: I think it's
given them incredible tools. So to reiterate on the
weather and climate modeling, in 2020, when we started
exploring the neural operators we had developed, it had
worked well on fluid dynamics, more as a proof of concept. And we wanted a real-world case. And we had collaborators in
the Lawrence Berkeley Lab, here, local. And this data set was open. And it was open sourced by
the European premier climate and weather organization, ECMWF. And so we're like,
OK, let's try this. And many people
discouraged us because this was considered
extremely difficult, high resolution,
hundreds of variables. And it has taken weather
scientists decades of work to carefully tune
physics-based models, which try to model from
ground up to get to where we are with the
current weather capabilities. But the beauty of deep learning
is as long as the data is there, we can just go try it. And even with our
first attempt, what we were surprised was
we got to good accuracy not only of typical weather
conditions like here in California, which honestly
isn't that hard to predict, but extreme weather events--
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – NYtQuneZMXc
text:
And even with our
first attempt, what we were surprised was
we got to good accuracy not only of typical weather
conditions like here in California, which honestly
isn't that hard to predict, but extreme weather events-- storms, hurricanes, typhoons. Because you don't think as
a machine learning scientist that you would get that
because they're rare events. In the last four
decades, the dataset maybe has a handful of these. But again, the reason why
I believe this was possible is because there are
clear physical signatures. Hurricanes have this hot and
air cool mixing together. So it is not just the amount
of data, it's the signatures and how significant
are these events. And the model is
able to pick that up and is able to even do now
better than traditional models. And I think that took a lot of
weather scientists by surprise. But I'm also very heartened
to see how much adoption has happened at lightning speed. Because ECMWF made
all these models. And our model and
Google DeepMind has done excellent work after
that with GraphCast and others. They're available. It built its own model. It declared them as operational,
which is the gold standard, meaning side by side,
it's running today with traditional models. And weather scientists
can make judgments. And on the other key is
because of the speed, we can now build much
larger statistical ensembles of events like hurricanes. Because what you care about
is not just one prediction of where the hurricane
is going to go, because there's a
lot of uncertainty. You look at perturbations
of the current conditions and see how many
of those tracks are going to land in Florida,
for instance, in a very specific region. So each city can then decide
should the evacuation happen. If it's 90% versus 60% chance,
it's a very different policy decision. But now we can enable
that which was not possible with
traditional models. So it's not just, OK, accuracy
is somewhat better and faster, but now we can go to
the tail events, which is the hardest aspect.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – NYtQuneZMXc
text:
decision. But now we can enable
that which was not possible with
traditional models. So it's not just, OK, accuracy
is somewhat better and faster, but now we can go to
the tail events, which is the hardest aspect. And we can come up with
accurate, calibrated probabilities and assess
risk for these events. JAMES MANYIKA: One
of the things that's been interesting to observe
in the last few years, one of the changes has been the
deeper collaborations that now happen between my
colleagues and I and others, but also with academia. So I think in almost
all of this work, there's deep collaboration
between AI researchers and scientists in
different disciplines, but also in academic
institutions. I think, for
example, some of you may have seen some
work we published last year on connectomics,
where we tried to do figure out the wiring
diagram of the brain, for example, at
the synapse level. That work was a 10-year
collaboration with the Lichtman Lab at Harvard, doing this very
painstaking foundational work. But I want to come
back, Pushmeet, you and I often spend a lot of
time talking about mathematics. You all heard about
AlphaEvolve yesterday. But I remember one of the
things that was striking was last year when AlphaGeometry
and AlphaProof did well enough to compete at the Math
Olympiad and compete at the gold level,
one of the things that was striking about that-- I don't know if
all of you tracked that-- was the fact that in a
lot of how it did what it did, it used a lot of its
own generated examples to train itself. Talk a lot about how you
think about the role of data and other things
that are critical and new techniques that are
emerging as we think about not just mathematics, because you
and I talk a lot about that, but also other areas of science. PUSHMEET KOHLI: So
I think when you we talk about AI and
machine learning, I think data and experience. We don't get
insights in a vacuum. So insights come
from experience. And experience, it
comes from data. And that data could be data
that scientists have collected
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – NYtQuneZMXc
text:
we talk about AI and
machine learning, I think data and experience. We don't get
insights in a vacuum. So insights come
from experience. And experience, it
comes from data. And that data could be data
that scientists have collected over many, many years, like we
did in the case of AlphaFold through the PDB, the
Protein Database Bank. Or it is experience
that has been generated through simulations. Now, across many
different applications, whether you look at biology
or another great example for synthetic data generation
is, in fact, fusion. So in fusion, if
you want to learn a policy that can stabilize
plasma in a tokamak, you have very limited data. Because those tokamak
fusion reactors run for a very short
duration of time. So you don't have
much data to train on. So in our work on the
model called Pulsar, where we were training a
policy to stabilize plasma in a tokamak, a
lot of the training happened on complete
simulation data. And the real challenge
was, how do you generalize? How do you make the
policy so robust that it can generalize and
go over the sim-to-real gap, simulation to real
domain transfer? That even though
that policy has been trained on simulated data, when
it is applied in a real reactor, it works. And that is what we showed
that it was able to do. Now, we have been
able to do this in a whole spectrum of
different applications, even, as you mentioned,
in mathematics, where it is particularly
sort of relevant, where in the case of alpha
geometry, for instance, we have very limited number
of geometry problems. Even though we have a
lot of geometry problems, but the appetite for these
models is immense, as you know. They are quite data hungry. So we had to generate
a large amount of data, in terms of synthetically
generated geometry problems, which we were
giving these models to solve. And that is how
the model actually learned itself to
solve and get itself much better to reach
at a gold level standard on geometry problems. On the other side,
there's another element,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – NYtQuneZMXc
text:
problems, which we were
giving these models to solve. And that is how
the model actually learned itself to
solve and get itself much better to reach
at a gold level standard on geometry problems. On the other side,
there's another element, which is test time model, which
was used in AlphaProof, which is when it tries to solve
these very hard problems-- and by the way, these are
incredibly hard problems. Even [INAUDIBLE]
medalists wouldn't be able to solve them
as a matter of fact. They would take time to
solve these problems. These are really problems at
the highest difficulty level. What the model was doing was
it was taking the problems and, like a human
mathematician, it was seeing if it can solve a
simpler variant of the problem. So suppose you have to prove
a theorem for all reals. So first you can
say, well, I'll not prove the theorem
for all reals, I'll prove the theorem for only
even numbers or odd numbers or a restricted domain. And then I can generalize. So this idea of really
synthetically generating a lot of variants of the
problem, trying to solve them, and in the process,
learning from that experience to then finally
be able to solve the larger, more difficult problem. So synthetic data generation
is everywhere in that sense. JAMES MANYIKA: And it's
also interesting to see how that mix changes for
different kinds of problems between real-world training
data versus synthetic versus simulated data. But I wanted to
come to something I know, Joelle, you spend
quite a bit of time thinking about, which is even as we
make all these advances, there's still important things
to think about, about how to be responsible and how to
think about responsibility in the science realm. What are some of the things you
think a lot about when you think about responsibility with
regards to AI and science, in particular? JOELLE BARRAL: And maybe to
echo what Pushmeet was saying, synthetic data obviously
is something very important as well from a
responsibility perspective. Because we really want
to make sure that what

in particular? JOELLE BARRAL: And maybe to
echo what Pushmeet was saying, synthetic data obviously
is something very important as well from a
responsibility perspective. Because we really want
to make sure that what we're building in
healthcare in particular, is going to help
everyone everywhere. And that's only possible
if the data we train on represents people everywhere. And that's really hard
because data is scarce and we don't necessarily
have it globally. And so something
we're doing with data, in particular, is to see
how synthetic data can help us augment. So if you have models that
have been trained only on a particular demographic,
you can perturb the data so that you're on a
bunch of dimension, checking what your model would
do on that perturbed version of a particular scenario. I talked about AMIE at the
beginning, our Articulate Medical Intelligence Explorer. And this is a system that
we're testing primarily with patient actors mimicking
what's done in medical school. Students learn
complicated scenarios with actors mimicking the
behavior of a patient going through a particular
diagnosis in the way they interact with
the physician. Because that's
really the only way to have something
rigorous methodologically and to be able to
grade those students. Did they react properly
when that patient actor forgot to mention
half of their symptoms or lied midway through
the encounter, et cetera? So the students really have to
learn and have been learning that way for decades now. So we used the similar
methodology with our models to also be able to
assess them responsibly. The other thing we're doing
on a responsibility front, which is really important
from my perspective, is to always engage
with the stakeholders and the external ecosystem. And in healthcare,
it means physicians from a variety of disciplines. It can be ethicists, of
course, the big hospital centers, et cetera. And that is also a way
for us to make sure that what we're building is
addressing real clinical needs

I'm struck by what you said about this idea of

title: Google I/O 2025 – NYtQuneZMXc
text:
of, how do we expand
our understanding of the physical
world, an AI that understands it in all of its
different levels of complexity? And of course, the
one big bottleneck, like Pushmeet also
mentioned, was the data. So if we have the
internet with all of it, we could just download
and train the model. It's much more straightforward. But even if we
know the equations in lots of these
physical systems, you can go down all the
way to quantum scale and say Schrodinger's
equation, but even to generate the fully accurate
result for about 100 electrons takes more than the
age of the universe. So all these systems,
in that sense, are extremely compute limited
and unrealistic to generate the full, accurate data
needed to train the models. But then the question is, what
levels of approximate data can we give the model? So you know this data
is wrong and there's going to be a sim-to-real gap. But first of all, in simulation
can you refine and give it more and more accurate data? So this notion of
curriculum and how do you hopefully have a lot
of data that is approximate and only a little bit
of the expensive data that's accurate to fine tune. And you can get
the model to behave as the fully accurate system. I think that's the next realm
in a lot of these areas. And for us, when it
comes to these kinds of multiscale phenomena
like fluid dynamics, we are now able to
establish, both theoretically and in practice as
well, that you can now train these models with solvers
that we know are approximate. So you can train them
on a coarse grid, and then you can use
the same AI model and fine tune it with the
fine grid information. Because the idea is if
you think of a hurricane and you only look at it as a
coarse scale, it gets blurry. And without capturing
the fine ideas, you can never get to the full,
accurate path of the hurricane. But now, if most of
the way you train with that kind of
coarse scale data, and with fine scale
with what happens in between those coarse grid,
what happens in between, if you
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – NYtQuneZMXc
text:
you can never get to the full,
accurate path of the hurricane. But now, if most of
the way you train with that kind of
coarse scale data, and with fine scale
with what happens in between those coarse grid,
what happens in between, if you can provide that to
these neural operators, they can refine internally. As opposed to what people
have been doing more traditionally is, let's
keep the coarse grid solver and try to correct the effects
with another machine learning model called a closure model or
you coarse grain your average out in some simple
way, add it up. But these are hand engineered. Mixing across scales doesn't
happen in an additive way like people think they do. They are bad approximations. But with neural operators, it
kind of has the flexibility to figure out how to
optimally mix across scales to fit to the target data and do
that with high data efficiency. JAMES MANYIKA: Pushmeet, what

in biology, in chemistry, in weather
prediction, in health. At the same time, we are
seeing these agents which can do hypothesis
generation, can operate at the
level of basically the scientific
process, Co-Scientist, AlphaEvolve, which really speed
up the scientific process. How do these two things come
together, where the agents now are able to also integrate these
deep, domain-specific systems and communicate across those
two very different kinds of cognitive processes? And so that acceleration
that comes from it, I think, is amazing. We are already seeing that in
the context of AlphaEvolve. AlphaEvolve was
able to discover-- it was applied on 50
open math problems. 75% of those problems, it
went to the state of the art. 20%, it went beyond
the state of the art.
